prompt: In the history of American land ownership and railroad development in Southern California, one figure stands out for his singular determination to maintain private control over a vast stretch of coastline. Frederick Hastings Rindge, born in 1857 in Cambridge, Massachusetts, made his fortune in the hardware business before moving to Southern California and acquiring a 13,000-acre ranch that encompassed nearly all of what is today the city of Malibu. The property stretched for miles along the Pacific coast, bounded by the Santa Monica Mountains to the north and the ocean to the south. For years, Rindge fought legal battles to keep trespassers, settlers, and most importantly the Southern Pacific Company off his land. The Southern Pacific sought to use the power of eminent domain to extend its coastal rail line through Rindge's property, which would have opened the isolated coastline to development. Rather than submit, Rindge conceived an audacious plan: he would build his own standard-gauge railroad along the coast, thereby preempting the Southern Pacific's claim by establishing an existing rail corridor. Construction began in 1905, the year of Rindge's death at age 48, and was continued by his widow May Knight Rindge, who became one of the wealthiest women in the state and proved equally determined to preserve the family's coastal empire. The railroad ultimately extended 15 miles along the coast, with tracks, bridges, and tunnels carved into the rugged Santa Monica Mountains. Engineers constructed concrete bridges over deep canyons and blasted tunnels through headlands that jutted into the sea. Despite its engineering ambition, the line never connected to any external rail network, never carried paying passengers, and was used primarily to haul supplies and livestock for the ranch. Storms, landslides, and the relentless corrosion of salt air gradually degraded the infrastructure, while the enormous legal costs of fighting condemnation proceedings gradually bankrupted the Rindge estate. By the 1920s, the State of California had won its own eminent domain case, and Roosevelt Highway — now Pacific Coast Highway — was built over and alongside much of the former rail bed, rendering the railroad obsolete. Today almost no trace of the railroad remains, though occasional concrete footings and tunnel portals can be spotted along the Malibu coast by hikers who know where to look. This short-lived, privately-built standard-gauge railroad, constructed entirely within the Rindge ranch to prevent Southern Pacific encroachment, was formally incorporated as the Hueneme, Malibu and Port Los Angeles

Christian virgin-martyr Olivia of Palermo, venerated since the Middle Ages as a local patron saint, has a hagiographic legend detailing her life. Accounts suggest she was born to a noble Sicilian family, either around 448 AD or in the 9th century. Dedicated to the Lord from an early age, she declined worldly honors and riches, instead giving charity to the poor. During a period of Vandal conquest in Sicily, she comforted imprisoned Christians and urged them to maintain their faith. Impressed by her unwavering spirit, the Vandals sent her to Tunis, hoping the governor there could break her resolve. In Tunis, she reportedly performed miracles and converted many to Christianity, leading to her banishment to a remote area. Despite being left among wild animals, they lived peacefully around her. While preclinical evaluations have highlighted its potential efficacy in models of depression, anxiety, and obsessive-compulsive disorder, it has also been observed to negatively affect cognition and memory. Heistadmoen, a Norwegian military encampment situated in Kongsberg, Buskerud county, has a history stretching back to 1909 when the Telemark Infantry Regiment established a presence there, utilizing buildings relocated from Gråtenmoen. Its initial strategic purpose was to enhance the defense of the Kongsberg Weapons Factory. During World War II, the site underwent significant development under German occupation, with most of the camp's structures being erected using the labor of Soviet prisoners of war. Following the war, from 1945, it served to house German prisoners, guarded by Allied forces. From 1946 to 1952, it facilitated the training of infantry battalions for the Tysklandsbrigaden, Norway’s contingent to the Allied occupation in Germany. The camp saw the formation of the new Battalion Telemark in 1994, which later relocated, as did His Majesty the King's Guard 6 Company. prompt: In September 1975, the Island Trees Board of Education received a list of books deemed inappropriate by Parents of New York United, leading them to temporarily remove these books from school libraries. Although a committee recommended returning most of the books, the Board overruled this decision, returning only two. This action prompted five high school and junior high school students to file a lawsuit, alleging a violation of their First Amendment rights. The District Court initially granted summary judgment in favor of the school board, citing their discretion, but the Second Circuit challenged and remanded this decision due to questions regarding the board's motives. The case, which centered on the removal of eleven specific books from school libraries and even a curriculum, ultimately reached the Supreme Court. Al-Salihiyya was a Palestinian Arab village located in the Safad Subdistrict, approximately 25 km northeast of Safad, at the confluence of the Jordan River and Wadi Tur'an. Traditionally, its inhabitants were associated with the Ghawarna, an exonym for people of the Hula Valley's drainage plains. During the late Ottoman period in 1881, the village was described as a "mud village" with around ninety Muslim residents on arable land near a marsh and river. By the British Mandate era, the population had grown to 1,281, almost entirely Muslim, inhabiting 257 houses according to the 1931 census. A 1936 visitor noted that the villagers, like others in the Huleh region, exhibited a "pronounced negroid element" and spoke a dialect close to Egyptian Arabic. Their primary industry involved harvesting papyrus from Lake Huleh to manufacture matting for both interior use and construction, creating weatherproof reed huts by layering mats. They also engaged in agriculture, cultivating wheat, Indian corn, and millet despite challenging invasive grass, and supplemented their livelihoods by fishing and guiding duck hunters. The village contained a mosque and an elementary school for boys. In the 1945 statistics, the population reached 1,520, all Muslims, owning 4,528 dunams of land. According to Israeli sources, the village had historically maintained a 'friendly' relationship with the Yishuv. However, an internal dispute arose in late May 1948 between younger villagers advocating cooperation with Jewish forces and elders fearing retribution from Arab armies if they did so. This internal conflict, coupled with unfulfilled negotiation attempts, led to the village's depopulation on May 25, 1948, by the Palmach's First Battalion during

The Republic of Croatia maintains an independent body responsible for upholding the nation's foundational legal document, distinct from the traditional branches of government. This institution is tasked with ensuring that all legislative acts and regulations align with the supreme law of the land, safeguarding the constitutional rights and freedoms of its citizens. Its functions are meticulously outlined in specific articles of the Croatian Constitution, as well as in dedicated legal acts and its own rules of procedure. The court supervises the compliance of political parties' activities with the Constitution, arbitrates jurisdictional disputes between governmental branches, and monitors the legality of elections and referendums. Furthermore, it determines the constitutionality of laws and regulations, even those no longer in effect, provided a request is filed within a year of their cessation. The Hallandsås Tunnel, an 8.7 km railway tunnel in southwestern Sweden, was conceived in the 1980s as a crucial component for upgrading the West Coast Line to double track. This ambitious project aimed to alleviate a bottleneck across the Hallandsås geological formation, which previously featured a single-track stretch with challenging curves, steep gradients, and numerous level crossings, causing significant delays and requiring detours for many trains. Construction commenced in 1992, with an initial completion target of 1995. However, the endeavor was plagued by unforeseen difficulties, including extensive groundwater ingress that hampered drilling efforts and led to a contractor bankruptcy. A subsequent scandal erupted when a poisonous sealing compound, Rhoca-Gil, containing acrylamide, was used, resulting in environmental damage and health issues for workers and local livestock, halting the project from late 1997 until 2005. Following remediation and a positive decision by the Riksdag, construction resumed, employing a sophisticated tunnel boring machine nicknamed "Åsa" that installed concrete lining segments and injected slurry. Born in Auckland, New Zealand, and of Samoan descent, Roy Asotasi emerged from the Marist Saints junior club, quickly establishing himself in professional rugby league. His career began with the Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs, where he secured two Jersey Flegg Cup premierships in 2000 and 2001 before making his NRL debut in 2002. Located in the Yvelines department of northwestern France, the railway station at Mantes-la-Jolie serves as a critical junction connecting the Paris to Le Havre railway with the line diverging towards Caen and Cherbourg. Its operational history dates back to May 9, 1843, when it commenced service as part of the line between La Garenne-Colombes and Rouen-Rive Gauche, established by the Compagnie du chemin de fer de Paris à Rouen. This initial line was subsequently integrated into the broader Paris to Le Havre railway network by 1847, while the distinct Mantes-la-Jolie to Cherbourg railway was progressively opened between 1855 and 1858. Both lines were later incorporated into the Chemins de fer de l'Ouest in 1855. Originating in the 10th and 11th centuries in Egypt, this monotheistic ethnoreligious group developed from Isma'ilism, a branch of Shia Islam, yet its adherents do not identify as Muslims. Their foundational text, the Epistles of Wisdom, incorporates diverse philosophical elements from Gnosticism, Neoplatonism, Pythagoreanism, and even Hinduism, alongside Christian and Islamic tenets, forming a distinct and often secretive theology that emphasizes the mind and truthfulness. Conversions are not permitted, as it is believed all current followers are reincarnations of an initial generation. While recognizing many prophets including Jesus and Mohammed, Jethro, Moses' father-in-law, is held in highest esteem, with his tomb near Tiberias being a crucial religious site. The community is broadly divided into the al-Juhhal, who do not access holy texts, and the al-Uqqal, who adhere to ascetic rulings and provide spiritual leadership. The formation of intricate patterns at the interface between two fluids, known as viscous fingering, was mathematically described in 1958 by Philip Saffman and G. I. Taylor. This phenomenon, which can occur in porous media like soils or within a Hele-Shaw cell, is primarily observed during drainage processes. A key condition for its emergence is the injection of a less viscous fluid into a more viscous one; the inverse scenario, where a more viscous fluid displaces a less viscous one, results in a stable interface without such pattern formation. Experimental studies frequently utilize Hele-Shaw cells, comprising two closely spaced, parallel glass sheets containing the viscous fluid. Within these cells, two common configurations are employed: the channel setup, where the less viscous fluid is injected at one end, and the radial setup, where it is injected at the center. In English architecture, particularly from the Tudor period onward, a banqueting house served as a distinct, pavilion-like structure, often accessed through gardens from the main residence. These buildings were solely dedicated to entertaining, with a specific focus on dining. They could be situated on the roof of a primary house, as seen in many 16th-century prodigy houses, or elevated to provide enhanced air circulation or scenic views, sometimes with a simple kitchen below, as exemplified at Hampton Court Palace. Characterized by rich ornamentation and frequently featuring numerous windows to capitalize on vistas, these structures typically contained a single grand room and lacked bedrooms. The term "banquet" during this era referred not only to a grand celebratory meal but also to a lighter repast taken in a specialized location away from the main dining area, with the latter meaning being pertinent to these garden structures. They were often used for serving dessert after large meals or for lighter fare like tea or snacks on pleasant days. During the early Cold War, the Soviet Union developed a series of seagoing base minesweepers, beginning design work in 1943 to incorporate lessons from earlier projects. The final concept for these vessels was completed by CKB-363 in 1946, leading to serial production starting in 1947 and yielding approximately 180 units across several variants. These ships were designed for a range of critical tasks, including identifying and clearing minefield boundaries, conducting reconnaissance sweeps, creating passages through minefields, and escorting other vessels. An additional operational requirement was the ability to detect and destroy submarines. In the early 1950s, Poland decided to undertake licensed construction of these ships in its own shipyards. Technical documentation for the Project 254K arrived in Poland in January 1953, with serial production approved in 1954. While early vessels were completed in the 254K configuration, subsequent orders shifted to the more advanced 254M variant, which featured enhanced armament and upgraded minesweeping equipment, including MKT-1 contact sweeps, BGAT acoustic sweeps, and TEM-52M electromagnetic sweeps.
